name: HOEPA
reg: Home Ownership & Equity Protection Act · TILA §32

items.text: Some loans cost enough that the law sets them apart and bolts on extra protection. HOEPA is that tripwire. Cross any one of three thresholds — on rate, on points and fees, or on the prepayment penalty — and the loan becomes a "high-cost" mortgage carrying a list of restrictions. The aim isn't to ban these loans but to make sure a borrower was warned and advised before signing. HOEPA sits inside TILA as Section 32, which is why you'll hear "Section 32 loan."

The first threshold watches the APR against the average prime offer rate. A first-lien loan goes high-cost when its APR beats APOR by 6.5 percentage points or more. That first-lien tripwire — APR over APOR by 6.5 percentage points or more — is the one to fix. A subordinate lien, or a personal-property loan under $50,000, crosses at the steeper 8.5 percentage points over APOR.

The other two triggers catch cost loaded elsewhere. On a loan of $27,592 or more (the 2026 figure), total points and fees that come to more than 5% of the loan amount make it high-cost — so once points and fees cross 5% of the loan amount of the loan amount, the loan is high-cost. A prepayment penalty does the same if it runs more than 2% of the amount prepaid, or can apply more than 36 months after consummation. Once a loan is high-cost, HOEPA demands pre-loan homeownership counseling, the high-cost disclosure must reach the borrower at least 3 business days before consummation, and any late fee is capped at 4% of the past-due payment.
